title: Break the cycle with weak_ptr
prompt_md:
## Goal

When a parent owns its children and each child points back at the parent, using
`shared_ptr` in *both* directions creates a reference cycle that leaks -- the
objects are never destroyed. Design the two node types so cleanup works: the
owning direction (parent -> children) keeps `shared_ptr`, and the back-pointer
(child -> parent) is a `std::weak_ptr`.

This must genuinely require `weak_ptr`: with a `shared_ptr` back-pointer the
parent's `use_count` would be wrong **and** none of the destructor lines would
ever print (the cycle leaks). The number of destructor lines depends on the input
child count, so a constant printer cannot reproduce the output either.

Define two types:

- `Parent` -- has an `int id`, owns its children in a
  `std::vector<std::shared_ptr<Child>>`, and prints `Parent <id> destroyed` in its
  destructor.
- `Child` -- has an `int id`, refers back to its parent via
  `std::weak_ptr<Parent>`, and prints `Child <id> destroyed` in its destructor.

## Input

- Line 1: the parent id `pid` and a child count `n` (1 <= pid <= 9999;
  1 <= n <= 20), separated by a space.
- Line 2: `n` child ids (each 1 <= cid <= 9999), space-separated.

## Output

After creating the parent (`shared_ptr`), creating `n` children owned by the
parent, and linking each child's weak back-pointer:

1. `owners of parent: <count>` -- the parent's `use_count()`. With weak
   back-pointers this is `1`.
2. for each child, in creation order: `child <cid> sees parent: <pid>` -- read
   through the child's back-pointer (lock it first).
3. `watch expired before: false` -- an independent `weak_ptr<Parent>` is not yet
   expired (use `std::boolalpha`).

Then release the local parent `shared_ptr`. Because the parent was the only owner,
it is destroyed, and destroying it destroys the children it owns:

4. `Parent <pid> destroyed`, then `Child <cid> destroyed` for each child in the
   order the parent's vector destroys them.
5. `watch expired after: true`.

## Notes

Use `weak_ptr` for exactly the back-pointer. A `shared_ptr` there would make
`owners of parent` print `<n+1>` and would suppress every destructor line.

test_cases.label: Three children
test_cases.expected_stdout:
owners of parent: 1
child 11 sees parent: 7
child 12 sees parent: 7
child 13 sees parent: 7
watch expired before: false
Parent 7 destroyed
Child 11 destroyed
Child 12 destroyed
Child 13 destroyed
watch expired after: true

test_cases.label: Single child
test_cases.expected_stdout:
owners of parent: 1
child 5 sees parent: 1
watch expired before: false
Parent 1 destroyed
Child 5 destroyed
watch expired after: true

test_cases.label: Four children
test_cases.expected_stdout:
owners of parent: 1
child 9 sees parent: 100
child 8 sees parent: 100
child 7 sees parent: 100
child 6 sees parent: 100
watch expired before: false
Parent 100 destroyed
Child 9 destroyed
Child 8 destroyed
Child 7 destroyed
Child 6 destroyed
watch expired after: true

title: Safe observer with weak_ptr::lock
prompt_md:
## Goal

A `std::weak_ptr` observes a `shared_ptr`-managed object without keeping it
alive, so the object may vanish while you still hold the observer. Build a small
command interpreter around one shared object and one weak observer, accessing the
object **only** through `lock()`.

Start with one owner (a `shared_ptr<int>` made with `std::make_shared`) holding
`value`, and a `weak_ptr<int>` observing it. Keep additional owners in a
container. Then process `q` commands:

- `copy` -- add another owner (a copy of an existing owner), if any owner remains.
- `drop` -- remove one owner, if any remain.
- `peek` -- `lock()` the observer; if it succeeds, print `value: <v>`; otherwise
  print `value: expired`.
- `count` -- print `owners: <n>` where `<n>` is the observer's `use_count()`.

## Input

- Line 1: two space-separated integers `value` and `q`
  (-100000 <= value <= 100000; 1 <= q <= 50).
- Next `q` lines: each is `copy`, `drop`, `peek`, or `count`.

## Output

- One line per `peek` and per `count` command, as described above. `copy` and
  `drop` produce no output.

## Notes

Never dereference the `weak_ptr` directly -- always go through `lock()`. Once
every owner is gone, the object stays expired.

test_cases.label: Lives then expires
test_cases.stdin:
42 7
peek
copy
count
drop
drop
peek
count
